ProjectDescription: The NPO is a community-driven urban accessibility platform that enables residents to report mobility hazards through a structured mobile form with GPS, photos, severity rating, and disability impact classification. The system aggregates this data into an intelligent Municipal Operations Dashboard, automated reports, and an AI-based severity index. Integrated with Google Maps for Accessible Route Planning, SafePath helps cities prioritize critical issues, optimize maintenance resources, and provide safer navigation for people with disabilities.

Lists.Name: Sprint 8
Lists.Description:
This is the final stabilization push. Your mission is to switch from feature building to a collaborative "Bug Hunting" phase alongside the entire team to ensure the platform is production-ready. 
 
 You are moving from the "feature" phase to the "quality" phase. This sprint is entirely focused on identifying glitches and ensuring the user experience is flawless before the final handoff. Since everyone is doing QA, you will monitor the Meeting Room for every "Bugs case" logged and address them in your dedicated branch. 
 
 Success means a pixel-perfect, error-free frontend that has been fully merged into the main repository.

Cards.Name: Infrastructure Audit & High-Level Data Modeling
Cards.Description:
Welcome to the team! 
 
 This week is your "Technical Landing": a low-pressure space to explore the project's foundations. 
 
 Your objective is to partner with the PM to translate their business journey into a technical blueprint. 
 
 You will verify the environment and create the "Big Picture" database model that will serve as our project's technical map.
ChecklistItems: Team Kickoff: Participate in the "Project Kickoff" to meet your teammates and understand the project’s high-level mission.
Local Repository Setup: Clone the provided GitHub repository to your local developer environment to begin your technical exploration.
Sprint Branch Initialization (Mentor Panel): Use the mentor buttons panel to create your official branch for the current sprint.
Local Sync Verification (Local Environment): From your local environment, push a "Health Check" commit to your branch to confirm your local IDE is perfectly synced with the repository.

GitHub Cycle Practice (Mentor Panel): Return to the mentor buttons panel to create a Pull Request (PR) and merge your branch into main. Use this to gain a hands-on understanding of the project's GitHub cycle.
Infrastructure Audit: Connect to the Postgres DB and perform a "Stress Test" (e.g., creating and deleting a dummy table) to ensure the environment is stable for future data.

Collaborative Journey & Persona Review: Sit down with the PM to review their Global Journey Diagram and review the UI/UX Designer's new User Personas. Use this session to brainstorm the core entities and relationships (e.g., Citizens, Disabled people, Municipal employee) needed to support the entire system.
High-Level Schema Diagram: Based on your review with the PM, create a visual ERD (Entity Relationship Diagram) showing the high-level data architecture.
Blueprint Centralization: Save your schema diagram in the Meeting Room so the team can reference our "Data Source of Truth." (Your Mentor can guide you on the process).

Cards.Name: Frontend Orientation & Environmental Foundation
Cards.Description:
Welcome to the front line! 
 
 We are thrilled to have you on board. 
 
 This first week is your "Technical Landing" - a low-pressure space to explore your new digital playground. 
 
 While the PM and UI/UX teams define the project's soul, your objective is to ensure your development environment is perfectly tuned and to collaborate on the project's global structure. 
 
 You are the team's bridge between design and code.
ChecklistItems: Team Kickoff: Participate in the "Sprint 1 Kickoff" to meet the team and align on our high-level mission.
Local Repository Setup: Clone the provided GitHub repository to your local developer environment to begin your technical setup.
Sprint Branch Initialization (Mentor Panel): Use the mentor buttons panel to create your official branch for the current sprint.

Local Sync Verification (Local Environment): From your local environment, push a "Health Check" commit to your branch to confirm your local IDE and build tools are perfectly synced with the repository.

GitHub Cycle Practice (Mentor Panel): Return to the mentor buttons panel to create a Pull Request (PR) and merge your branch into main. Use this to gain a hands-on understanding of the project's GitHub cycle.
Tech Stack Audit: Explore the project's frontend boilerplate (React/Next.js/etc.) and verify that all necessary dependencies and linting rules are correctly installed.
Collaborative Journey Review: Sit down with the PM and the Backend Developer to review the Global Journey Diagram. Use this to identify potential UI complexities or navigation challenges early.

Global Styling Foundation: Based on the UI/UX Designer's initial research, begin initializing the project's "Foundations"—set up the global CSS variables for the color palette, typography scales, and spacing units.

Cards.Name: Ecosystem & CRM Foundation
Cards.Description:
Welcome to the team!

We are so excited to have you as our Urban Accessibility Research Lead.

This first week is a low-pressure opportunity for you to learn about the urban mobility and accessibility ecosystem.

Your mission is to map out the "playing field" – the network of city infrastructure, accessibility challenges, and stakeholders (e.g., municipalities, community organizations, advocacy groups, and tech partners) – and identify who the key players are.

Think of the AI Mentor as your research consultant who will guide you while you explore how citizen reporting, municipal operations, and accessibility data flow through the SafePath platform.

This week is all about discovery and building a solid knowledge base to help the team make SafePath as safe, inclusive, and effective as possible.
ChecklistItems: Team Kickoff: Join the "Sprint 1 Kickoff" to meet the team and understand our shared starting point.
Anchor Alignment: Start an introductory conversation with the AI Customer to understand the business world they operate in every day.
CRM & Research Setup: Ask the AI Mentor for a step-by-step walkthrough of the CRM module and for professional guidance on creating a "Stakeholder Map."

Industry Discovery: Conduct "Desk Research" on the urban accessibility and mobility ecosystem to understand how it works (e.g., how accessibility hazards are reported, managed by municipalities, and experienced by people with disabilities).
Competitive Analysis: Review 2-3 existing global solutions and summarize how they approach this industry.

Stakeholder Mapping: Identify the key groups involved (citizens reporting hazards, municipal officials, community organizations, and accessibility advocacy groups) and input these entities into your project mapping or CRM screen.
NSM Prep: Get familiar with the term NSM (Next Sprint Meeting); you’ll share your ecosystem findings here to help the team stay grounded in the industry's reality.

Cards.Name: Vision, Discovery & The PRD Foundation
Cards.Description:
Welcome to the helm!

Your mission this week is to transform initial ambiguity into a solid strategic roadmap.

You are the Strategic Detective, digging deep into the "Why" and "Who" of this project.

The Big Picture: While we are establishing the foundations now, keep in mind that the actual construction of our first module – starting with the Mobile Accessibility Reporting Interface – begins in Sprint 3.

This week is about building the trust and the blueprint: the Project PRD, required to make that build successful.
ChecklistItems: Project Kickoff: Lead the official "Project Kickoff" in the meeting room. Set the vision, align the team on the mission, and facilitate introductions.
Kickoff Synthesis: Publish a concise meeting summary and a list of immediate action items in the Team Chat to ensure Day 1 alignment.
Relationship Building: Initiate introductory conversations with each teammate (BE, FE, UI/UX, BizDev) to establish a personal connection and a supportive team culture.
Team Connection Recap: Post a high-level summary of the team’s collective strengths and support needs in the Team Chat to reinforce the collaborative foundation.
PRD Initialization: Initialize the Product Requirements Document (PRD). This is the central home for the project’s DNA. Use it to document every strategic decision you make this week.

Problem Validation: Interview the AI Customer to confirm reality vs. assumptions regarding their current workflow. Document their "Pain Points" and "Success Criteria" in the Discovery section of your PRD.
Customer Insight Broadcast: Share the key takeaways and prioritized action items from the customer interview in the Team Chat to ground the team in the user's reality.
North Star & KPIs: Define the project's North Star Metric and 3–5 supporting KPIs. Add these to the PRD to serve as the decision filter for the entire journey.

Actor Alignment: Identify the main actors (e.g., Reporting Citizens, Municipal Call Center Representatives, Field Inspectors, Accessibility Coordinators, and City Administrators). Map out their primary responsibilities and “Needs” within the User Persona section of the PRD.

Journey Mapping & Centralization: Create a visual diagram of the end-to-end urban accessibility reporting and resolution journey. Integrate this diagram directly into the PRD so it serves as the project’s visual anchor.
PRD Accessibility: Save the PRD in the Squad Room. Ensure it is available to everyone and clearly marked as the project's "Source of Truth."
Team Journey Demonstration: Set a meeting with the entire team to demonstrate the Journey Map and walk through the Draft PRD. Gather feedback and ensure everyone sees the "Big Picture."
Vision Alignment Summary: Publish the final meeting summary and a consolidated list of feedback-driven action items in the Team Chat to officially close the loop on Sprint 1.

Cards.Name: Ecosystem Discovery & Research Foundation
Cards.Description:
Welcome to the team! 
 
 As our lead designer, you are the architect of the user's experience. 
 
 This first week is your "Discovery" phase: a low-pressure space to understand the industry and define who we are building for. 
 
 Before diving into the visuals, you will coordinate your research roadmap with the Product Manager. 
 
 Your goal is to gather the competitive intelligence and persona insights needed to shape a high-impact design strategy in the weeks ahead.
ChecklistItems: Project Kickoff: Participate in the Project Kickoff meeting to align on the mission and meet your new teammates.
Product Manager Alignment: Meet with the Product Manager to plan your specific research tasks and milestones for the remainder of the sprint.
Figma Setup: Initialize your Figma project and link it to the Meeting Room. Ask the AI Mentor if you need instructions on how to sync the files.

Market Research: Analyze existing solutions in related fields (e.g., municipal service request systems, reporting platforms, civic issue tracking apps, or smart city management tools). Note the pros and cons of each and upload a summary to the Meeting Room.
Visual Inspiration Board: Create a Mood Board or "Inspiration Table" in Figma. Focus on UI patterns, color psychology, and layouts that align with the project goals.

User Personas: Define initial User Personas (e.g., Reporting Citizen, Municipal Dispatcher, Field Inspector, Accessibility Coordinator). Document their specific needs, motivations, and potential pain points. Add persona designs to the project's Figma.
User Journey Insights: Review the Global User Journey created by the Product Manager. Flag any specific areas or flows that require extra UX attention.

Cards.Name: Data Architecture & Technical Readiness
Cards.Description:
This week is about definition over execution.

Your goal is to move from the abstract "Big Picture" to a precise technical blueprint for the Mobile Accessibility Reporting Experience.

You are the architect of the data flow.

By the end of this week, you will have established the plan for how the Frontend and Backend communicate hazard reports, photos, locations, and severity data.

You aren't building the final logic yet; you are preparing the "manual" so that the team can build with total confidence in Sprint 3.
ChecklistItems: Environment Launch: Launch the sprint and create your Sprint 2 branch via the mentor buttons panel to begin your technical exploration.

Tooling and Library Audit: Review your current backend stack and libraries (e.g., validation libraries, database drivers). Ensure everything is optimized and ready for the specific data-entry and form-handling tasks coming next week.

Frontend Data Sync (General Handshake): Meet with the Frontend Developer to agree on the general "Blueprint" for how data will move between the UI and the server (e.g., Auth patterns, error handling, and endpoint structure).

Requirements Sync (Tables and Fields): Once the PM has finalized the User Story, meet to identify the specific Tables and Fields required to support the Accessibility Reporting Interface. Map out exactly what data points need to be stored (e.g., exact address, accessability type etc.).

Database Schema Blueprint: Update the Database Schema diagram based on the PM's finalized fields. This acts as the "Contract" for the project's data structure and serves as the team's single source of truth.

Mock Data Provision: Generate a set of "dummy" Accessibility Reporting data. The goal is to give the Frontend Developer realistic data to work with in Sprint 3 before you have built the actual database logic.
Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to present your technical blueprint. Confirm that your architecture can support the validation rules required by the customer.

Blueprint Delivery (PR and Merge): Commit your final schema designs and mock data files to your branch. Create a Pull Request (PR) and merge it into main via the mentor buttons panel to lock in the technical blueprint for the build.

Cards.Name: UI Architecture & Component Scaffolding
Cards.Description:
This week, you are the "Architect" of the interface.

Before we start coding the actual logic for the Accessibility Reporting Interface in Sprint 3, you will build the reusable "Atoms" that make development fast and consistent.

You are building the "lego pieces" and the "hallways" of the application.

By the end of the week, the team should be able to click through the basic navigation of the flow, even if the forms don't "save" data yet.

This is where we build the skeleton so that next week, we can add the muscle.
ChecklistItems: Environment Launch: Launch the sprint and create your Sprint 2 branch via the mentor buttons panel to begin your technical exploration.
Component Library Initialization: Set up a dedicated workspace for your reusable UI components (e.g., a /components library folder or a Storybook setup).

Atomic Component Build: Build the foundational, "logic-less" components required for the Accessibility Reporting Form: Buttons, Input Fields (Text/Select), Loaders, and Success Modals. Apply the Visual Language (Colors and Typography) established by the Designer this week.

Frontend Data Sync: Meet with the Backend Developer to agree on the "Data Blueprint." Confirm the specific "Request" (what the form sends) and "Response" (what the server says back) so that your components are ready for real data.

App Navigation "Hallways": Set up the empty page containers and the URL paths for the Accessibility Reporter flow (e.g., /submit for the form and /success for the confirmation). This ensures the structural journey is ready for the logic in Sprint 3.
UI/UX Handshake: Review the Designer’s final design. Ensure general synchronization regarding technical feasibility and confirm that the layout aligns with the UI components you are preparing.
Milestone: Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to present your component library and technical readiness. Show the team the "UI Atoms" and the clickable "Hallways" you’ve built.

UI Delivery (PR & Merge): Commit your foundational components and navigation routes to your branch. Create a Pull Request (PR) and merge it into main via the mentor buttons panel. This locks in the UI architecture before the build phase begins.

Cards.Name: Accessibility Hazard Reporting Interface
Cards.Description:
This week, you serve as the project’s Strategic Pulse.

Your mission is to maintain a dual-stream focus: Internal Alignment to deeply understand the product’s technical evolution, and External Validation to ground that progress in community needs.

You are responsible for ensuring that the Accessibility Reporting Interface is not just a technical achievement, but a user-friendly solution for citizens to report hazards.

You will bridge the gap between the development team’s potential constraints and the actual reporting habits of community members.
ChecklistItems:
User Engagement: At the start of the week, consult with the AI Customer to identify "unknowns" regarding accessibility reporting. 
 Focus: Clarify operational details that will affect the interface design (e.g., "What types of accessibility issues do citizens most frequently report, and how do they prefer to describe them?").

Internal Product Alignment: Once the PM and UI/UX Designer have drafted the initial User Story and design, meet to conduct a deep-dive. 
 Goal: Ensure you can articulate exactly how the reporting fields and validations are intended to work before you head into the community.

Field Stakeholder Outreach: Conduct targeted interviews with 2-3 potential users (e.g., local residents, disability advocates, or community organizers). 
 Validation: Present the high-level concept of the reporting interface to gather raw, unbiased feedback.

Operational Habit Validation: Analyze whether the proposed reporting workflow (specifically the predefined fields and photo upload) aligns with the "on-the-ground" behavior of citizens reporting hazards.

Data Management Logging: Use the CRM button in the Meeting Room to document all field insights and stakeholder profiles. 
 Data Management: Ensure you are managing the report list (entering/editing) so the entire team has a "Data Source of Truth" regarding reported Accessibility issues.

Strategic Resource Management: Use the Resources button to upload and link relevant research, community feedback, or case studies. 
 Benchmark: Ensure the team has access to these references for inspiration.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Present your "Field Intelligence" at the NSM. 
 Prioritization: Help the team prioritize reporting fields and validation logic based on real-world urgency versus technical "nice-to-haves."
Sprint 4 Staggered Look-Ahead: Work with the PM to identify the key stakeholders needed for the Accessibility Reporting Interface validation, which will be your primary focus next week.

Cards.Name: Accessibility Hazard Reporting Alignment
Cards.Description:
This week, you are the Bridge between the high-level reporting process and the actual build.

Your mission is to translate the vision from Sprint 1 into a detailed, actionable User Story for the Accessibility Reporting Interface.

Sprint 2 is about removing every technical and logical blocker before we start coding in Sprint 3.

You are the team’s Unlocker.

We are focusing entirely on the "Reporting Foundation" - capturing the needs of diverse citizens reporting accessibility issues like broken pavement, missing ramps, lack of wheelchair access access and other urban issues.

Success means a citizen can seamlessly report an issue, upload relevant photos, and submit their report via a mobile-optimized form.

By the end of this week, the team must know exactly what to build, why, and how.

Every decision you document now regarding the "Reporting Experience" ensures the developers and designers can execute the build with total focus and zero ambiguity.
ChecklistItems: Meeting Orchestration: Schedule a short Sprint 2 Status Meeting at the start of the week for team alignment. Schedule the Next Sprint Meeting (NSM) for the second half of the week.
Status Sync Recap: Publish a concise summary of the week’s setup tasks and any immediate blockers identified during the status meeting to the Team Chat.

Customer Validation: Interview the AI Customer to finalize the required Reporting Form fields (limit to max 6) and identify the specific IF/THEN validation rules, such as rules for specific hazard or barrier types or severity ratings.

The User Story Deep-Dive: Locate the existing Accessibility Reporting Interface card in the "User Stories" Trello list and fill it out completely: 
 (1) Description Field: Write the formal story ("As a... I want... so that...") , define the In-Scope/Out-of-Scope lists, and include a Data Table listing every Field Name, Field Type (Text, Number, etc.), and whether it is Required (Yes/No). 
 (2) Acceptance Criteria Checklist: Populate the pre-made, empty checklist on the card with specific "Done" conditions, for example, "Confirmation message appears" or "Mandatory field errors trigger correctly."

Design Alignment: Sync with the UI/UX Designer to review the Reporting Form Design and success screens. Ensure the final design, including the visual language, is saved in the Squad Room before the sprint ends.

Technical Requirements Sync: Meet with the Backend Developer once your User Story is drafted. Help them identify the specific Tables and Fields needed to support the Reporting Form logic based on your Data Specification.
Technical Readiness Audit: Verify the development environment. Confirm the BE has the Schema ready and has generated Mock Data, and that the FE has built the UI Atoms and Navigation Hallways.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Lead the planning session. 
 Agenda: 
 (1) Present the User Story, Data Specs, and AC 
 (2) Facilitate T-shirt sizing (S/M/L) 
 (3) Confirm developer understanding 
 (4) Assign owners
Sprint 3 Launch Sync: Publish a consolidated summary of the build goals and a clear list of prioritized action items in the Team Chat to formally trigger the start of the execution phase.

Cards.Name: Accessibility Hazard Reporting Interface
Cards.Description:
This week, you will focus on creating a streamlined reporting process for citizens to report accessibility barriers in urban environments.

Your mission is to transform user feedback into a structured reporting interface that ensures clarity and consistency in data collection.

You are the Guardian of Accessibility. By the end of this week, the developers should have a "Design Source of Truth" including the final visual elements, layout, and user experience for the Accessibility Reporting Interface.

Your goal is to ensure the interface not only functions effectively but also feels intuitive, reliable, and accessible from the very first interaction.
ChecklistItems: Logo & Visual Language: Interview the AI Customer regarding the product name, logo guidelines, and overall visual direction. Design the product logo and establish the visual language in Figma (e.g., Color Palette and Typography).
Customer Requirement Sync: Interview the AI Customer to identify specific user needs for the Accessibility Reporting Interface and verify any functional expectations.

PM Requirement Sync: Meet with the PM as soon as the Accessibility Reporting Interface user story is drafted. Review the required data fields and validation logic to ensure your design supports all business and technical constraints.
Resident Reporting Form Design: Create a mobile-first design of the reporting form in Figma. Focus on layout hierarchy and ease of use on smaller screens to ensure a smooth reporting experience.

States & Notifications: Design success and error states for all form fields and add them to the Figma design system. Create the corresponding success and error notifications for the report submission flow.

Technical Handshake: Sync with the Frontend Developer to review your designs. Ensure general synchronization regarding technical feasibility and confirm that your UI components align with the project’s component library.
Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to present the final design to the team. This will serve as the "North Star" for the upcoming development in Sprint 3.
Sprint 4 Look-Ahead: Briefly coordinate with the Product Manager to identify the core requirements for the Municipal Operations Dashboard module. This will be your research focus starting next week.

Cards.Name: Feature Logic & API Execution
Cards.Description:
This is it. The Build Week.

You are no longer just an architect. You are the Engine Specialist.

Your mission is to build the interface that allows citizens to report accessibility issues effectively.

By the end of this sprint, when a user clicks "Submit" on the reporting form, your backend must capture that data, validate it against the established criteria, and store it securely for municipal use.

You are responsible for ensuring that the data "Accessibility Reporting Interface" is secure, accurate, and ready for analysis and response.
ChecklistItems: Sprint 3 Launch: Participate in the Sprint Meeting led by the PM. Review the User Story and Acceptance Criteria to ensure you are 100% aligned on the build goals.
Environment Setup: Create your Sprint 3 branch via the mentor buttons panel to begin the build.
Database Migration: Implement the physical tables in the database based on the schema you finalized in Sprint 2. Ensure all fields (IDs, timestamps, and data types) are correctly configured.
Validation Engine Build: Code the IF/THEN logic as defined in the PM's User Story (e.g., ensuring the issue type is selected and severity is rated appropriately).

API Endpoint Development: Build the POST endpoint that will receive the input data. Ensure the response body includes all necessary parameters for UI responsiveness (e.g., specific error codes, unique report IDs, or updated state objects).
Unit Testing: Write and execute unit tests for your validation logic and database service. Ensure that "bad data" is rejected with the correct error codes and "good data" is prepared for storage.
Peer Code Review: Share your code with the AI Mentor for a review session to ensure your logic is clean, secure, and follows best practices.

Integration Test (Internal): Use your "Mock Data" from last week to test the endpoint. Confirm that valid reports are saved and invalid submissions trigger the correct error response codes before involving the frontend.

Frontend Handshake: Pair with the Frontend Developer to "wire up" the reporting form. Verify that their UI components are successfully sending the data to your endpoint and receiving the expected response parameters.

Next Module Requirements Sync: Meet with the PM once the Municipal Operations Dashboard User Story is drafted to identify the specific tables and fields needed to support the upcoming logic based on the new Data Specification.
Technical Readiness Audit: Prepare the environment for the PM's audit by ensuring the Municipal Operations Dashboard schema is ready and comprehensive mock data has been generated for the next phase.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to plan the development of the Municipal Operations Dashboard. This session serves as the primary planning phase for the upcoming sprint, though it can also be used to demonstrate that the hazard reports are being successfully captured and stored.
Feature Shipping (PR & Merge): Push your final code, create a Pull Request (PR), and merge it into main via the mentor buttons panel to officially complete the Accessibility Reporting Interface.

Cards.Name: Accessibility Hazard Reporting Interface Logic & API Integration
Cards.Description:
This is the Build Week.

You are no longer just building components. You are building the Experience.

Your mission is to take the "Atoms" from Sprint 2 and wire them into a fully functional, responsive reporting form that handles user input and server feedback.

By the end of this sprint, a user should be able to fill out the resident reporting form, see real-time validation, and receive a clear success or error message from the server.

You are responsible for the "Handshake" between the user and the data engine.
ChecklistItems: Sprint 3 Launch: Participate in the Sprint Meeting led by the PM. Review the User Story and Acceptance Criteria to ensure the reporting interface behavior matches the business requirements.
Environment Setup: Create your Sprint 3 branch via the mentor buttons panel to begin the build.

Temporary Access Point: Add an "Accessibility Reporting Interface" button to the index.html file "frontend-buttons" panel. This ensures the form is accessible to all team members via the "Live Project" link during development. Note that a professional Landing Page will be properly designed toward the end of the project (Sprint 7).
Form Logic Implementation: Transform your static components into a dynamic reporting form. Implement the local state management to capture user input for all fields defined in the PM's Data Table.
Client-Side Validation: Code the immediate UI feedback logic (e.g., preventing empty submissions, checking severity ratings, or ensuring correct photo uploads) before the data even hits the server.
API Integration (The Wiring): Connect your reporting form to the Backend's POST endpoint. Implement the fetch or axios calls to send the user data to the server.

Responsive Feedback & Error Handling: Use the server's response parameters to trigger UI changes. Build the logic for loading states, specific field error messages, and the redirect to the Success "Hallway."
Peer Code Review: Share your code with the AI Mentor for a review session to ensure your state management is efficient and your UI logic is robust.
Integration Test (Internal): Run a full end-to-end test of the flow. Confirm that the reporting form correctly handles both a "Happy Path" (success) and "Edge Cases" (validation errors).
Backend Handshake: Pair with the Backend Developer to verify that your "Request" body perfectly matches their endpoint's expectations and that you are correctly parsing their "Response" parameters.

Next Module Requirements Sync: Meet with the PM once the Municipal Operations User Story is drafted to identify any unique UI requirements, table structures, or navigation hallways needed for the next phase.
Technical Readiness Audit: Prepare the environment for the PM's audit by ensuring the Municipal Operations Dashboard atoms and navigation hallways are built and ready for the Sprint 4 integration.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to plan the development of the Municipal Operations Dashboard. This session serves as the primary planning phase for the upcoming sprint, though it can also be used to demonstrate the live reporting flow and successful navigation to the confirmation screen.
Feature Shipping (PR & Merge): Push your final code, create a Pull Request (PR), and merge it into main via the mentor buttons panel to officially complete the Reporting Form Experience.

Cards.Name: Strategic Pulse and Management Validation
Cards.Description:
This week, you serve as the project’s Strategic Pulse.

Your mission is to maintain a dual-stream focus: Internal Alignment to deeply understand the next phase of the Municipal Operations Dashboard’s technical evolution, and External Validation to ground the upcoming dashboard features in real-world operational needs.

You are responsible for ensuring that the management experience is not just a technical dashboard, but a tool that fits the high-speed operational needs of city officials overseeing accessibility and hazard reports.

You will bridge the gap between the development team’s potential constraints and the actual operational habits of city officials, maintenance teams, and community stakeholders.
ChecklistItems: Customer Sync: At the start of the week, consult with the AI Customer to identify "unknowns" regarding the Municipal Operations Dashboard. Clarify management details that will affect the build, such as how officials prefer to navigate hazard reports and detailed data views.

Internal Product Alignment: Once the PM and UI/UX Designer have drafted the initial User Story and design for the Municipal Operations Dashboard, meet to conduct a deep-dive. Ensure you can articulate exactly how the dashboard logic and status transitions are intended to work.

Field Stakeholder Outreach: Conduct targeted interviews with 2-3 potential "City Officials" or "Maintenance Coordinators" (e.g., city staff, accessibility advocates, or community leaders). Present the high-level concept of the management dashboard to gather raw, unbiased feedback.

Operational Habit Validation: Analyze whether the proposed digital management workflow (specifically the data filtering and task assignment) aligns with the "on-the-ground" behavior of officials managing reported barriers or hazards.

CRM Intelligence Logging: Use the CRM button in the Meeting Room to document all management-related insights and stakeholder profiles. Ensure you are managing the prospect list so the team has a "Data Source of Truth" regarding the needs of internal users.

Strategic Resource Management: Use the Resources button to upload and link relevant market research regarding municipal dashboards or management software. Ensure the team has access to these benchmarks for inspiration.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Present your "Field Intelligence" at the NSM. Help the team prioritize dashboard features and business logic based on real-world operational urgency versus technical "nice-to-haves."
Sprint 5 Staggered Look-Ahead: Work with the PM to identify the key stakeholders needed for the validation of the Accessible Route Planning module, which will be your primary focus next week.

Cards.Name: Tactical Blueprint and User Story
Cards.Description:
This week, you are the Bridge between the current build and the next phase of the project.

Your mission is to translate the vision from the global roadmap into a detailed, actionable User Story for the Municipal Operations Dashboard.

Sprint 3 is about removing every technical and logical blocker before we start coding in Sprint 4.

You are the team’s Unlocker.

We are shifting focus from data entry to data management, exploring how city officials will interact with, process, and oversee the accessibility barrier or hazard reports flowing into the system.

Success means defining a clear, actionable workflow that transforms raw data into organized, manageable outcomes.

By the end of this week, the team must know exactly what to build for the next module, why, and how.

Every decision you document now regarding the Management Experience ensures the developers and designers can execute the build with total focus and zero ambiguity.
ChecklistItems: Meeting Orchestration: Schedule a short Sprint 3 Status Meeting at the start of the week for team alignment. Schedule the Next Sprint Meeting (NSM) for the second half of the week.
Status Sync Recap: Publish a concise summary of the week’s setup tasks and any immediate blockers identified during the status meeting to the Team Chat.

Customer Validation: Interview the AI Customer to finalize the required management views (Accessibility issues header vs. Detailed View) and identify the specific business logic for the Task Assignment flow.

The User Story Deep-Dive: Locate the existing Municipal Operations Dashboard card in the “User Stories” Trello list and fill it out completely: 
 (1) Description Field: Write the formal story (“As a... I want... so that...” ), define the In-Scope/Out-of-Scope lists, and include a Data Table listing every Field Name (e.g., Issue Type, Severity Score, District), Data Type, and required User Actions (e.g., Assign Task, Update Status). 
 (2) Acceptance Criteria Checklist: Populate the pre-made, empty checklist on the card with specific “Done” conditions, such as functional data filtering and sorting or validated logic for task assignments and status updates.

Design Alignment: Sync with the UI/UX Designer to review the dashboard interface designs and detail views. Ensure the final design, including the visual language, is saved in the Squad Room before the sprint ends.

Technical Requirements Sync: Meet with the Backend Developer once your User Story is drafted. Help them identify the specific Tables and Fields needed to support the Municipal Operations Dashboard logic based on your Data Specification.

Technical Readiness Audit: Verify the development environment. Confirm the BE has the Schema ready and has generated Mock Data, and that the FE has built the UI Atoms and Navigation Hallways for the dashboard.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Lead the planning session. 
 Agenda: 
 (1) Present the User Story, Data Specs, and AC 
 (2) Facilitate T-shirt sizing (S/M/L) 
 (3) Confirm developer understanding of the management logic 
 (4) Assign owners for Sprint 4
Sprint 4 Launch Sync: Publish a consolidated summary of the build goals and a clear list of prioritized action items in the Team Chat to formally trigger the start of the execution phase.

Cards.Name: Detailed Design and Management Experience
Cards.Description:
This week, you expand the visual ecosystem.

Your mission is to move from the core brand elements of Sprint 2 into a high-fidelity dashboard experience for the Municipal Operations Dashboard.

You are the Guardian of the User Experience.

By the end of this week, the developers should have a "Design Source of Truth" including the layouts for the reported issues, detail views, and interactive management elements.

Your goal is to ensure that complex data feels organized, clear, and easy to act upon from any device, maintaining the professional trust established by the brand.
ChecklistItems: Customer Requirement Sync: Interview the AI Customer to identify user priorities, management workflows, and specific data points required for the Municipal Operations Dashboard.

PM Requirement Sync: Meet with the PM as soon as the Municipal Operations Dashboard user story is drafted. Review the defined acceptance criteria and data hierarchy to ensure your dashboard design aligns with the administrative workflow.

Dashboard & Detail Design: Create the high-fidelity design for the management interface in Figma. Ensure the layout includes both the reported issues overview (for high-level tracking) and the detailed report items (for granular hazard details).

Action States & Feedback: Design the interactive elements for the management flow, such as buttons for task assignments and visual feedback for status updates. Ensure these states follow the established visual language and color palette.

Technical Handshake: Sync with the Frontend Developer to review your designs for the upcoming module. Ensure general synchronization regarding technical feasibility and confirm that the UI components align with the established design system.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to present the final Municipal Operations Dashboard designs to the team. This will serve as the "North Star" for the upcoming development in Sprint 4.

Sprint 5 Look-Ahead: Briefly coordinate with the Product Manager to identify the core requirements for the Accessible Route Planning module. This will be your research and design focus starting next week.

Cards.Name: Dashboard Logic and Data Retrieval
Cards.Description:
This week, you transition from the data collection phase to the operational oversight phase.

You are no longer just aggregating reports. You are the Dashboard Management Specialist.

Your mission is to build the retrieval systems and logic gates that allow city officials to monitor, filter, and manage reported accessibility issues.

By the end of this sprint, your backend must be able to serve complex data sets, including both summary metrics and detailed issues reports, to the dashboard and execute status updates based on operational actions.

You are responsible for ensuring that the Municipal Operations Dashboard foundation is robust, performant, and ready to support the operational workflow.
ChecklistItems: Sprint 4 Launch: Participate in the Sprint Meeting led by the PM. Review the User Story and Acceptance Criteria to ensure you are 100% aligned on the dashboard build goals.
Environment Setup: Create your Sprint 4 branch via the mentor buttons panel to begin the build.

Database Migration/Updates: Implement any structural changes or new fields required for reported issues tracking and management views. Ensure indexes are correctly set for efficient filtering and sorting.
Query Logic Build: Code the retrieval logic to fetch both high-level issues summaries and detailed reports. Ensure the data is structured to support the visualizations defined in the PM's User Story.

API Endpoint Development: Build the GET endpoints for the dashboard and detailed views, alongside the PATCH/PUT endpoints to handle status updates and task assignments. Ensure the response body includes the updated state objects for UI responsiveness.
Unit Testing: Write and execute unit tests for your retrieval logic and status update functions. Ensure that unauthorized or invalid state changes are rejected with the correct error codes.
Peer Code Review: Share your code with the AI Mentor for a review session to ensure your logic is clean, secure, and follows best practices.

Integration Test (Internal): Use your "Mock Data" from Sprint 3 to verify the dashboard endpoints. Confirm that the filters return the correct data and that status updates persist in the database before involving the frontend.

Frontend Handshake: Pair with the Frontend Developer to "wire up" the dashboard. Verify that your endpoints are serving the correct JSON structure for the Accessibility issues summary and detailed views and responding correctly to user actions.

Next Module Requirements Sync: Meet with the PM once the Accessible Route Planning User Story is drafted to identify the specific tables and fields needed to support the upcoming logic based on the new Data Specification.
Technical Readiness Audit: Prepare the environment for the PM's audit by ensuring the Accessible Route Planning schema is ready and comprehensive mock data has been generated for the next phase.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to plan the development of the next operational features. This session serves as the primary planning phase for the upcoming sprint, though it can also be used to demonstrate the functional dashboard logic and data processing.
Feature Shipping (PR & Merge): Push your final code, create a Pull Request (PR), and merge it into main via the mentor buttons panel to officially complete the Municipal Operations Dashboard.

Cards.Name: Municipal Operations Dashboard Interface
Cards.Description:
This week, you transition from data collection to data orchestration.

You are no longer just building input forms. You are building the Dashboard Experience.

Your mission is to take the dashboard components and navigation pathways from Sprint 3 and wire them into a fully functional interface for city officials to monitor and manage reported accessibility barriers.

By the end of this sprint, a user should be able to navigate a dynamic list of Accessibility issue, drill down into detailed reports, and execute status updates through responsive action buttons.

You are responsible for ensuring that complex data sets are rendered clearly and that every administrative action provides immediate, reliable feedback.
ChecklistItems: Sprint 4 Launch: Participate in the Sprint Meeting led by the PM. Review the User Story and Acceptance Criteria to ensure the dashboard behavior and action logic match the business requirements.
Environment Setup: Create your Sprint 4 branch via the mentor buttons panel to begin the build.

Temporary Access Point: Add a "Municipal Operations Dashboard" button to the index.html file "frontend-buttons" panel. This ensures the dashboard interface is accessible to all team members via the "Live Project" link during development. As a reminder, the final professional Landing Page will be designed in Sprint 7.

Dashboard Logic Implementation: Transform your static table and list components into a dynamic interface. Implement state management to handle the display of reported Accessibility issue and the "drill-down" logic for detailed views.
Filtering & Sorting Engine: Code the client-side logic for data organization. Ensure users can seamlessly filter and sort the reported issues based on the parameters defined in the PM's User Story.
API Integration (The Wiring): Connect your dashboard to the Backend’s GET endpoints for list/detail views and the PATCH/PUT endpoints for status updates.

Responsive Action Handling: Build the logic for interactive feedback. Ensure that clicking to update status triggers the correct loading states and updates the UI in real-time based on the server's response.
Peer Code Review: Share your code with the AI Mentor for a review session to ensure your logic is clean, secure, and follows best practices.
Integration Test (Internal): Run a full end-to-end test of the dashboard flow. Confirm that the dashboard correctly handles data rendering, filtering, and the successful execution of status updates.
Backend Handshake: Pair with the Backend Developer to verify that your data requirements for the reported issues view match their response structure and that action parameters are correctly aligned.

Next Module Requirements Sync: Meet with the PM once the Accessible Route Planning User Story is drafted to identify any unique UI requirements, map components, or navigation pathways needed for the next phase.
Technical Readiness Audit: Prepare the environment for the PM’s audit by ensuring the Accessible Route Planning components and navigation pathways are built and ready for the Sprint 5 integration.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to plan the development of the Accessible Route Planning module. This session serves as the primary planning phase for the upcoming sprint, though it can also be used to demonstrate the live dashboard flow, showing the navigation and the successful management of reported Accessibility issue.

Feature Shipping (PR & Merge): Push your final code, create a Pull Request (PR), and merge it into main via the mentor buttons panel to officially complete the Municipal Operations Dashboard experience.

Cards.Name: Strategic Pulse and Accessibility Validation
Cards.Description:
This week, you serve as the project’s Strategic Pulse. 
 
 Your mission is to maintain a dual-stream focus: Internal Alignment to deeply understand the product’s technical evolution into accessibility routing, and External Validation to ground the upcoming Accessible Route Planning module in real-world usability. 
 
 You are responsible for ensuring that the routing experience is not just a technical map, but a functional tool that fits the real-time operational needs of users navigating urban environments safely. 
 
 You will bridge the gap between the development team’s potential constraints and the actual mobility habits of users with accessibility challenges.
ChecklistItems: Customer Sync: At the start of the week, consult with the AI Customer to identify "unknowns" regarding the Accessible Route Planning module. Clarify accessibility details that will affect the build, such as how users with disability define their routes and what Accessibility issues data they need while navigating.

Internal Product Alignment: Once the PM and UI/UX Designer have drafted the initial User Story and design for the Accessible Route Planning module, meet to conduct a deep-dive. Ensure you can articulate exactly how the routing logic and hazard evaluations are intended to work.

Field Stakeholder Outreach: Conduct targeted interviews with 2–3 potential "Users" or "Accessibility Advocates" (e.g., individuals with mobility challenges, community organizers, or accessibility consultants). Present the high-level concept of the routing interface to gather raw, unbiased feedback.

Operational Habit Validation: Analyze whether the proposed digital routing workflow aligns with the "on-the-ground" behavior of users with disabilities navigating around accessibility hazards or barriers during their journeys.

CRM Intelligence Logging: Use the CRM button in the Meeting Room to document all accessibility-related insights and stakeholder profiles. Ensure you are managing the prospect list so the team has a "Data Source of Truth" regarding the needs of the users.

Strategic Resource Management: Use the Resources button to upload and link relevant market research regarding accessibility routing software, hazard reporting standards, or urban navigation benchmarks. Ensure the team has access to these references for inspiration.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Present your "Field Intelligence" at the NSM. Help the team prioritize routing features and Accessibility awareness based on real-world operational urgency versus technical "nice-to-haves."
Sprint 6 Staggered Look-Ahead: Work with the PM to identify the key stakeholders needed for the validation of the Reporting and Analytics module, which will be your primary focus next week.

Cards.Name: Tactical Blueprint and User Story
Cards.Description:
This week, you are the Bridge between the current build and the next logistical phase of the project.

Your mission is to translate the vision from the global roadmap into a detailed, actionable User Story for the Accessible Route Planning module.

Sprint 4 is about removing every technical and logical blocker before we start coding the routing features in Sprint 5.

You are the team’s Unlocker.

We are shifting focus from internal data management to user navigation, exploring how geospatial data and Accessibility reports will be used to facilitate safe urban travel.

Success means defining a clear, actionable workflow that transforms user-defined source and destination data into an optimized, Accessibility-aware route for the team.

By the end of this week, the team must know exactly what to build for the next module, why, and how.

Every decision you document now regarding the Accessibility Experience ensures the developers and designers can execute the build with total focus and zero ambiguity.
ChecklistItems: Meeting Orchestration: Schedule a short Sprint 4 Status Meeting at the start of the week for team alignment. Schedule the Next Sprint Meeting (NSM) for the second half of the week.
Status Sync Recap: Publish a concise summary of the week’s setup tasks and any immediate blockers identified during the status meeting to the Team Chat.

Customer Validation: Interview the AI Customer to finalize the required route planning views (e.g., Route Overview vs. Accessibility Alerts) and identify the specific business logic for Accessibility-aware route generation and navigation instructions.

The User Story Deep-Dive: Locate the existing Accessible Route Planning card in the “User Stories” Trello list and fill it out completely: 
 (1) Description Field: Write the formal story ("As a user... I want... so that..."), define the In-Scope/Out-of-Scope lists, and include a Data Table listing every Field Name (e.g., Source, Destination, Reported Accessibility issues), Data Type, and required User Actions. 
 (2) Acceptance Criteria Checklist: Populate the pre-made, empty checklist on the card with specific "Done" conditions relevant to the module’s core logic and technical requirements.

Design Alignment: Sync with the UI/UX Designer to review the interface designs for map integration and barriers visualization. Ensure the final design, including the visual language, is saved in the Squad Room before the sprint ends.

Technical Requirements Sync: Meet with the Backend Developer once your User Story is drafted. Help them identify the specific Tables and Fields needed to support the Accessible Route Planning logic and barriers data interactions.

Technical Readiness Audit: Verify the development environment. Confirm the BE has the Schema ready and has generated Mock Data, and that the FE has built the UI Atoms and Navigation Hallways for the Accessibility-aware map views.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Lead the planning session. 
 Agenda: 
 (1) Present the User Story, Data Specs, and AC 
 (2) Facilitate T-shirt sizing (S/M/L) 
 (3) Confirm developer understanding of the routing logic 
 (4) Assign owners for Sprint 5
Sprint 5 Launch Sync: Publish a consolidated summary of the build goals and a clear list of prioritized action items in the Team Chat to formally trigger the start of the execution phase.

Cards.Name: Detailed Design and Logistics Experience
Cards.Description:
This week, you translate the accessibility roadmap into a high-utility interface for users.

Your mission is to design the mobile-first "Accessible Route Planning Interface" for the Accessible Route Planning module.

You are the Guardian of the User Experience.

By the end of this week, the developers should have a "Design Source of Truth" that defines how users interact with their route planning, from barrier alerts to navigation instructions.

Your goal is to ensure that the interface is glanceable and functional, providing users with the essential information needed to navigate urban environments safely without unnecessary complexity.
ChecklistItems: User Requirement Sync: Interview the AI Customer to identify user priorities, essential accessibility data points, and the preferred workflow for navigating between source and destination.

PM Requirement Sync: Meet with the PM as soon as the Accessible Route Planning user story is drafted. Review the defined reported-barriers, routing logic, and navigational requirements to ensure your mobile framework is aligned with the operational plan.

User Interface Design: Create the high-level mobile design for the route planning experience in Figma. Design Focus: Establish the visual framework for navigating safe routes, including hazard or barriers markers and summaries of route options.

Accessibility Interaction States: Design the interactive elements for the user’s workflow, such as alerts for hazards and navigation triggers. Ensure these actions provide immediate visual confirmation using the established color palette.

Technical Handshake: Sync with the Frontend Developer to review your designs. 
 Synchronization: Confirm the technical approach for integrating barrier-location data and Google Maps Directions API. 
 Feasibility: Ensure the UI components are optimized for mobile performance and accessibility.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to present the final Accessible Route Planning designs to the team. 
 Agenda: 
 (1) Present the High-Level User Interface and Route layouts 
 (2) Demonstrate the core Accessibility-aware navigation flow 
 (3) Confirm design handoff for Sprint 5 execution
Sprint 6 Look-Ahead: Briefly coordinate with the Product Manager to identify the core requirements for the Reporting and Analytics module. This will be your research focus starting next week.

Cards.Name: Accessible Route Planning
Cards.Description:
This week, we enhance urban navigation for SafePath users.

You are no longer just a developer; you are the Accessible Route Planning Specialist.

Your mission is to build the logic that transforms user-defined routes into safe navigation paths by avoiding reported accessibility barriers/hazards.

By the end of this sprint, your module must be able to process source and destination inputs, interface with the Google Maps Directions API, and provide optimized walking or rolling route directions that dynamically account for hazards or places that lack proper accessibility.

You are responsible for ensuring that the Accessible Route Planning foundation is reliable and provides the necessary outputs for real-time accessibility-aware navigation.
ChecklistItems: Sprint 5 Launch: Participate in the Sprint Meeting led by the PM. Review the User Story and Acceptance Criteria to ensure you are 100% aligned on the route planning goals.
Environment Setup: Create your Sprint 5 branch via the mentor buttons panel to begin the build.

Database Migration/Updates: Implement any structural changes or new fields required for accessibility issues reporting and route planning views. Ensure necessary performance optimizations are implemented for efficient data retrieval.

Routing Logic Build: Conduct a technical deep-dive into the Google Maps Directions API documentation to identify the parameters required for incorporating pre-collected location data into route planning. Code the logic to pass the stored reported Accessibility barriers coordinates to the routing engine so routes can avoid problematic areas. Consult with the AI Mentor to ensure the external service integration is secure and performant.

API Endpoint Development: Build the GET endpoint that calls the Google Maps Directions API to generate an accessible route between two locations while considering the stored accessibility issue locations in the system. The endpoint should return the calculated route so it can be displayed in the user interface.
Unit Testing: Write and execute unit tests for your routing logic and hazard detection functions. Ensure that route processing and hazard avoidance are handled gracefully.
Peer Code Review: Share your code with the AI Mentor for a review session to ensure your logic is clean, secure, and follows best practices.
Integration Test (Internal): Use your "Mock Data" from Sprint 4 to verify the routing endpoints. Confirm that the Accessibility-aware logic returns the expected route directions.
Frontend Handshake: Pair with the Frontend Developer to "wire up" the navigation interface. Verify that your endpoints are serving the correct data structure for route display.

Next Module Requirements Sync: Meet with the PM once the Reporting and Analytics User Story is drafted to identify the specific tables and fields needed to support the upcoming logic based on the new Data Specification.
Technical Readiness Audit: Prepare the environment for the PM's audit by ensuring the Reporting and Analytics schema is ready and comprehensive mock data has been generated for the next phase.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to plan the development of the Reporting and Analytics module. This session serves as the primary planning phase for the upcoming sprint, though it can also be used to demonstrate the functional routing logic.
Feature Shipping (PR & Merge): Push your final code, create a Pull Request (PR), and merge it into main via the mentor buttons panel to officially complete the Accessible Route Planning module.

Cards.Name: Accessible Route Planning Interface
Cards.Description:
This week, the interface is set to enhance urban navigation.

Your mission is to build the mobile-first Accessible Route Planning interface for the Accessible Route Planning module.

You are no longer building for a desktop dashboard; you are building for users in the field who need high glanceability and one-handed interaction.

By the end of this sprint, your interface must be able to render a safe route based on user-defined start and end points and provide real-time navigation updates.

You are responsible for ensuring that the routing flow is intuitive, responsive, and robust enough to accommodate users with mobility challenges.
ChecklistItems: Sprint 5 Launch: Participate in the Sprint Meeting led by the PM. Review the User Story and Acceptance Criteria to ensure the routing behavior matches the user’s navigation needs.
Environment Setup: Create your Sprint 5 branch via the mentor buttons panel to begin the build.

Temporary Access Point: Add an "Accessible Routing" button to the index.html file "frontend-buttons" panel. This ensures the routing interface is accessible to all team members via the "Live Project" link during development. As a reminder, the final professional Landing Page will be designed in Sprint 7.
Mobile Route Implementation: Transform your routing atoms into a dynamic, mobile-optimized list. Implement state management to handle the display of the safe route sequence.
Map & Navigation Integration: Build the logic to receive and render navigation URLs dynamically from the Google Maps Directions API once the route is generated.
API Integration (The Wiring): Connect your interface to the Google Maps Directions API for route data.
Field Action Handling: Build the logic for mobile status transitions. Ensure that the UI provides clear, immediate feedback when a route is updated.
Peer Code Review: Share your code with the AI Mentor for a review session to ensure your mobile responsiveness is efficient.
Integration Test (Internal): Run a full end-to-end test of the routing flow on a mobile viewport. Confirm that the safe route renders correctly.
Backend Handshake: Pair with the Backend Developer to verify that the data and route information in the JSON structure match your interface requirements.

Next Module Requirements Sync: Meet with the PM once the Reporting and Analytics User Story is drafted to identify any unique UI requirements, data visualization needs, or navigation hallways for the next phase.
Technical Readiness Audit: Prepare the environment for the PM’s audit by ensuring the Reporting and Analytics UI atoms and navigation hallways are built and ready for the Sprint 6 integration.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to plan the development of the Reporting and Analytics module. This session serves as the primary planning phase for the upcoming sprint, though it can also be used to demonstrate the live routing flow and mobile field interactions.
Feature Shipping (PR & Merge): Push your final code, create a Pull Request (PR), and merge it into main via the mentor buttons panel to officially complete the Accessible Route Planning experience.

Cards.Name: Strategic Pulse and Analytics Validation
Cards.Description:
This week, you transition from the physical realities of city streets to the strategic intelligence of the municipal data room.

Your mission is to maintain a dual-stream focus: Internal Alignment to deeply understand how we aggregate the data collected from the previous modules, and External Validation to ensure the Reporting and Analytics module provides actual, actionable value to municipal operations teams, urban planners, and accessibility offices.

You are responsible for ensuring that the reporting experience is not just a collection of tables, but a powerful tool for tracking accessibility issues, identifying infrastructure patterns, evaluating response performance, and prioritizing urban improvements.

You will bridge the gap between technical data aggregation and the real-world decision-making habits of city officials, maintenance departments, accessibility coordinators, and municipal budget planners.
ChecklistItems: Customer Sync: At the start of the week, consult with the AI Customer to identify "unknowns" regarding the Reporting and Analytics module. Clarify which metrics (e.g., Accessibility Hotspot Density, Dynamic Severity Index, or Response & Resolution Performance) carry the most weight for municipal decision-making and infrastructure prioritization.

Internal Product Alignment: Once the PM and UI/UX Designer have drafted the initial User Story and design for the Reporting and Analytics module, meet to conduct a deep-dive. Ensure you can articulate the strategic utility of the reporting architecture, specifically how the interplay between filtering parameters and structured accessibility report data generates high-impact operational insights for municipal planning and infrastructure management.

Strategic Stakeholder Outreach: Conduct targeted interviews with 2–3 municipal officials, accessibility coordinators, or urban planners. Present the high-level concept of the Dynamic Severity Index, Accessibility Hotspot Density, and Response & Resolution Performance reports to determine if these analytics align with their operational and strategic reporting needs.

Data-Driven Habit Validation: Analyze whether the proposed reporting format (tabular vs. graphical) fits the existing "on-the-ground" behavior of managers who currently use spreadsheets or manual logs for data analysis.

CRM Intelligence Logging: Use the CRM button in the Meeting Room to document all analytics-related insights and stakeholder profiles. Ensure you are managing the prospect list so the team has a "Data Source of Truth" regarding the reporting needs of organizational leadership.

Strategic Resource Management: Use the Resources button to upload and link relevant research regarding municipal reporting best practices, accessibility data visualization benchmarks, or infrastructure impact metrics. Ensure the team has access to these references for inspiration when designing the Reporting and Analytics module.
Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Present your "Analytics Intelligence" at the NSM. Help the team prioritize report types and visualization styles based on real-world urgency versus technical complexity.
Sprint 7 Staggered Look-Ahead: Work with the PM to identify the key stakeholders needed for the validation of the next phase of the project roadmap, which will be your primary focus next week.
